option: Brutalism — raw unstyled-looking HTML energy: system fonts or harsh grotesks, exposed structure, no decoration, stark black-on-white
best_for: Portfolios, zines, dev tools, and brands whose whole point is anti-polish honesty
avoid_when: Any audience that reads rawness as brokenness — e-commerce checkouts and healthcare lose trust here
tradeoffs: Unmistakable and cheap to build; the line between deliberate and unfinished is thin, so typography and spacing discipline carry everything

option: Neo-brutalism — brutalism domesticated: thick black borders, hard offset shadows, saturated flat color blocks, chunky rounded corners
best_for: Consumer SaaS, fintech-for-young-users, and marketing sites wanting loud friendly confidence
avoid_when: Premium/luxury positioning or dense enterprise UI — the cartoon boldness caps how serious it can read
tradeoffs: Extremely current and screenshot-friendly; trend-tied enough that it will date visibly, and the hard shadows need consistent light direction or it falls apart

option: Swiss / International Style — strict grid, generous whitespace, one grotesk family, asymmetric composition, zero ornament
best_for: Agencies, architecture, publications, and brands wanting timeless typographic authority
avoid_when: Products needing warmth or playfulness — the style is deliberately impersonal
tradeoffs: Never dates and scales beautifully; demands real typographic skill since there is nothing else on the page to hide behind

option: Bauhaus-geometric — primary color blocks, circles/triangles/squares as compositional elements, geometric sans
best_for: Design-literate brands, education, museums, and creative tools honoring constructivist heritage
avoid_when: Subtle or understated brand voices — geometry this loud is a statement, not a backdrop
tradeoffs: Instantly art-historical and distinctive; easy to tip into poster-pastiche if the shapes decorate rather than structure the layout

option: Art-deco — symmetric geometric ornament, metallic accents, high-contrast display type with decorative details
best_for: Hospitality, events, cocktail/restaurant brands, and luxury with a heritage angle
avoid_when: Modern-tech positioning or dense functional UI — deco ornament fights information density
tradeoffs: Signals glamour and occasion like nothing else; ornament must stay in display/chrome layers, body UI keeps modern bones underneath

option: Editorial / magazine — display serif headlines, multi-column rhythm, pull quotes, generous art-directed imagery, print pacing
best_for: Publications, content-led brands, storytelling landing pages, and premium blogs
avoid_when: App-like interactive products — print pacing collapses when everything is a control
tradeoffs: Reads as authored and considered; requires genuinely good content and imagery since the style showcases rather than masks them

option: Kinetic typography — type itself is the hero visual: oversized headlines, scroll-driven movement, variable-font weight play
best_for: Portfolios, campaign pages, product launches, and brands with strong verbal identity
avoid_when: Content requiring sustained reading or any context where motion sensitivity matters unmitigated
tradeoffs: Zero-imagery distinctiveness — the words are the art direction; needs prefers-reduced-motion fallbacks and restraint or it becomes a demo reel

option: Collage / zine — torn edges, mixed media, tape/sticker artifacts, typewriter-meets-marker type, deliberate misregistration
best_for: Music, fashion drops, counterculture brands, and event promos wanting DIY authenticity
avoid_when: Anything needing perceived reliability (finance, health) — the aesthetic celebrates imperfection
tradeoffs: Impossible to mistake for a template; hard to systematize into a reusable component library, so best on campaign surfaces over product UI

option: Glassmorphism — frosted-glass panels: background blur, translucency, hairline light borders over colorful backdrops
best_for: Dashboards-as-hero-shots, macOS/iOS-adjacent products, and dark UIs wanting layered depth
avoid_when: Low-end device audiences (backdrop-filter is GPU-hungry) and text-heavy surfaces where translucency kills contrast
tradeoffs: Elegant depth without hard shadows; every glass layer needs a contrast-checked fallback and the backdrop must be interesting or the glass shows nothing

option: Claymorphism — inflated 3D-soft shapes: big radii, double inner/outer shadows, pastel candy palette
best_for: Kids/education products, friendly onboarding flows, and playful consumer apps
avoid_when: Serious/professional contexts and dense UI — clay eats space and reads toy-like by design
tradeoffs: Instantly approachable and huggable; the inflated look needs room, so it caps information density hard

option: Neumorphism — soft extruded-from-background controls via paired light/dark shadows, minimal color
best_for: Niche showcase pieces and single-purpose utilities (calculators, players) with few controls
avoid_when: Accessibility-critical products — the style's whole mechanism produces low contrast between control and background
tradeoffs: Distinctive softness nothing else replicates; fundamentally fights WCAG, so every interactive state needs a non-shadow signal added back

option: Material-depth — elevation as meaning: consistent shadow scale mapping z-height to importance, ink ripples, bold surfaces
best_for: Android-adjacent products, complex apps needing systematic layering rules a team can follow
avoid_when: Brands wanting to not look like Google — unmodified Material is one of the most recognizable defaults alive
tradeoffs: The elevation system genuinely aids comprehension; must be re-skinned (color, type, shape) or it reads Google-template

option: Skeuomorphic accents — selective real-world texture: a leather nav, paper cards, physical switches — on otherwise flat bones
best_for: Products with a strong physical-world metaphor (reading, music, journaling) wanting warmth
avoid_when: System-wide application — full skeuomorphism died for maintenance and density reasons; accents only
tradeoffs: One textured signature element creates huge memorability (UX048 energy); textures need retina-quality assets and dark-mode variants, real production cost

option: Bento grid — dashboard-of-cards composition: mixed-size rounded tiles in a tight lattice, each tile one fact/feature
best_for: Feature overviews, personal sites, and product pages summarizing heterogeneous content
avoid_when: Linear narratives — bento fragments storytelling into disconnected boxes by design
tradeoffs: Handles wildly mixed content gracefully and reads current; risks Apple-keynote sameness unless tile contents get real art direction individually

option: Data-dense dashboard — information-first: tabular figures, sparklines, tight line-height, minimal chrome, every pixel earns rent
best_for: Trading, analytics, ops tooling, and power-user products where density is the feature
avoid_when: Consumer onboarding or marketing — density before trust is built reads as overwhelm
tradeoffs: Experts genuinely prefer it and it signals seriousness; demands typographic precision (tabular nums, aligned columns) or density becomes noise

option: Industrial / utility — shipping-label aesthetics: mono labels, visible borders, stencil type, safety-color accents, no softness
best_for: Logistics, dev-infra, hardware, and field-service brands wanting honest tool energy
avoid_when: Comfort-seeking consumer contexts (wellness, hospitality) — utility reads cold there
tradeoffs: Ownable in B2B where competitors ship corporate-clean; the borders-everywhere approach needs a strict grid or it reads cluttered

option: Dark-technical — near-black layered surfaces, single electric accent, mono details, precise hairlines (the Linear-era product look)
best_for: Dev tools, AI products, and pro-audience SaaS wanting modern technical credibility
avoid_when: Mass-market or older audiences where dark UI reads as hard to see; also the fastest-saturating look in SaaS right now
tradeoffs: Currently the highest-credibility product aesthetic for technical buyers; so widespread that palette/type alone won't differentiate — needs a signature element on top

option: Terminal / mono — all-monospace, phosphor green/amber on black options, ASCII decoration, blinking-cursor affordances
best_for: CLI-adjacent tools, hacker-culture brands, security products, and retro-computing plays
avoid_when: Long-form reading and non-technical audiences — mono body text at length is measurably harder to read
tradeoffs: Total commitment version of dark-technical with stronger nostalgia pull; the gimmick ceiling is real, so functional surfaces usually need a proportional escape hatch

option: Luxury-minimal — vast whitespace, hairline rules, small refined type, muted palette, few words, no visible UI enthusiasm
best_for: High-end fashion, jewelry, architecture, and premium services where restraint is the signal
avoid_when: Feature-rich products needing explanation — luxury-minimal refuses to explain, which loses complex sales
tradeoffs: Whitespace as wealth signaling works; conversion elements (CTAs, forms) fight the aesthetic, so commerce needs careful exception design

option: E-ink / paper — warm paper white, near-black text, zero saturated accents, hierarchy purely from type weight and rules
best_for: Reading apps, writing tools, docs, and calm-tech products (palette twin of UX090)
avoid_when: Conversion-driven pages needing a CTA to visually outrank content
tradeoffs: Deepest-calm aesthetic available and inherently anti-template; demands flawless typography since type is the only design material left

option: Flat 2.0 — flat surfaces with pragmatic concessions: subtle shadows for interactive layers only, solid colors, simple geometry
best_for: General-purpose product UI wanting clean modern neutrality without strong stylistic commitment
avoid_when: Brand-forward surfaces that need memorability — flat 2.0 is deliberately unmemorable middle ground
tradeoffs: Safe, fast, accessible baseline; it is the default look this skill's distinctiveness module exists to push past, so pair with a signature element or palette

option: Corporate-clean — soft radii, gentle shadows, blue-family trust palette, illustration system, generous friendly spacing
best_for: Enterprise sales-led products, banking, insurance — contexts where familiar equals trustworthy
avoid_when: Anywhere differentiation matters more than reassurance — this is the most templated look in B2B
tradeoffs: Deliberately unthreatening and procurement-friendly; distinctiveness must come from copy voice and one owned element or it is invisible

option: Playful-rounded — everything-is-a-pill geometry, bouncy motion, saturated cheerful palette, chunky friendly type
best_for: Consumer social, food delivery, kids-adjacent, and any brand selling fun
avoid_when: Professional/expert tools — the aesthetic caps perceived seriousness on purpose
tradeoffs: Warmth and approachability are immediate; system needs the roundedness applied with discipline (one radius token, not per-component whims) to stay considered

option: Memphis — 80s design-group revival: squiggles, confetti shapes, clashing brights, patterned chaos on a controlled grid
best_for: Event brands, creative-tool marketing, and youth campaigns wanting deliberate visual noise
avoid_when: Sustained product UI — Memphis is a campaign/party aesthetic, exhausting at daily-use dosage
tradeoffs: Unmistakably energetic and fun; the controlled grid underneath is what separates it from actual chaos, and it dates as fast as it delights

option: Y2K — chrome gradients, bubble type, iridescent surfaces, early-web optimism artifacts (bevels, glows, sparkles)
best_for: Gen-Z consumer brands, music/fashion, and nostalgia-driven campaigns
avoid_when: Trust-critical or timeless-seeking contexts — Y2K is a dated-on-purpose statement with a shelf life
tradeoffs: Strong generational resonance right now; gradient/glow abuse tanks readability fast, so text layers need protected contrast zones

option: Vaporwave — pink/cyan dusk palette, grid horizons, classical-statue irony, VHS artifacts, deliberate kitsch
best_for: Music, streetwear, and internet-culture brands fluent in the irony
avoid_when: Any brand that means things sincerely — vaporwave's whole register is ironic distance
tradeoffs: Deep subculture credibility with the right audience; completely alienating outside it, and sincerity attempts in this skin read as missing the joke

option: Retro-futurism — space-age optimism: atomic shapes, orbit lines, warm oranges with deep browns, rounded technical forms
best_for: Aerospace-adjacent, coffee/beer packaging-led brands, and products selling optimistic futures
avoid_when: Cutting-edge-tech positioning — retro-future says 'the future as imagined then', not 'the future now'
tradeoffs: Warmly distinctive with built-in storytelling; the palette and shape language must stay period-coherent or it collapses into generic vintage

option: Organic / hand-crafted — irregular blob shapes, hand-drawn strokes and underlines, textured grain, humanist warmth
best_for: Wellness, sustainability, artisan food, and indie products countering algorithmic slickness
avoid_when: Precision-signaling domains (fintech dashboards, security) where wobble undermines accuracy
tradeoffs: The strongest available antidote to AI-generated sameness; hand-drawn assets must be genuinely custom — stock squiggles defeat the entire point

option: Maximalism — dense layered composition: patterns on patterns, multiple type families, saturated everything, controlled sensory overload
best_for: Fashion, entertainment, and editorial statements where more-is-more is the brand argument
avoid_when: Task-focused UI and accessibility-sensitive contexts — cognitive load is the aesthetic's raw material
tradeoffs: When it works it is unforgettable and unclonable; the hardest style to execute — requires a strong underlying grid and hierarchy or it is just clutter

option: Gradient-mesh / aurora — soft multi-color mesh backgrounds, floating luminous blobs, airy glass-adjacent foreground
best_for: AI products, creative tools, and modern SaaS marketing wanting optimistic energy without hard edges
avoid_when: Dense functional UI — mesh backgrounds behind data are noise; also rapidly becoming the default AI-product look
tradeoffs: Beautiful and current with low asset cost (pure CSS/SVG); saturation across the industry means it needs a distinctive palette or signature element to not read template
